messages.content:
The 2nd Estate protested the procedures of the Estates General and declared themselves the National Assembly and stormed out of the meeting.


The 2nd Estate protested the procedures of the Estates General and declared themselves the National Assembly and stormed out of the meeting.


True

False

False.

It was actually members of the Third Estate who protested the procedures of the Estates-General, not the Second Estate. The Estates-General was convened in May 1789, and it was the first such assembly called by the French monarchy in 175 years.

The traditional voting structure of the Estates-General was by estate, where each estate—First (clergy), Second (nobility), and Third (commoners)—had one vote. The Third Estate, representing the vast majority of the French population, demanded that voting be by head, which would give them more influence. When this was denied, and deadlock ensued, they broke away and, on June 17, 1789, declared themselves the National Assembly.

On June 20, 1789, members of the Third Estate, finding their usual meeting hall closed and locked, relocated to an indoor tennis court. Here they took the Tennis Court Oath, vowing not to disband until they had given France a new constitution.

The Second Estate comprised the French nobility, who initially had aligned themselves with the First Estate (the clergy) to maintain the status quo, but many of them eventually joined the Third Estate. The creation of the National Assembly was a pivotal moment in the French Revolution, leading to the eventual downfall of the Ancien Régime and the assertion of a new political order.
Place these events in Napoleon's life in order
Certainly, please provide me with the list of events related to Napoleon's life that you would like to have placed in chronological order.
joins radical jacobins boen on the island of corisca won a scholarshop at a military training school napoeloeon seizes control of frnace

The events you've listed are key moments in Napoleon Bonaparte's life. Here's the chronological order:

1. Born on the island of Corsica - Napoleon was born on August 15, 1769, in Corsica to Carlo Buonaparte and Letizia Ramolino.

2. Won a scholarship at a military training school - Napoleon was admitted to a French military school in 1779. He was eventually sent to the École Militaire in Paris, where he completed a two-year course in one year, graduating in 1785 at the age of 16.

3. Joins radical Jacobins - After the French Revolution began, Napoleon associated himself with the Jacobins, a political group that later became radicalized, which helped advance his military career. This association took place in the early 1790s.

4. Napoleon seizes control of France - Finally, in the coup of 18 Brumaire (November 9, 1799), Napoleon overthrew the French Directory, replacing it with the French Consulate, effectively ending the French Revolution and leading France himself. He became First Consul, giving him almost dictatorial powers, and later crowned himself Emperor Napoleon I in 1804.

Education was revamped and changed to three levels under Napoleon.


Education was revamped and changed to three levels under Napoleon.


True

FalseEducation was revamped and changed to three levels under Napoleon.


Education was revamped and changed to three levels under Napoleon.


True

False

True

Napoleon significantly revamped the French education system. Under his rule, education was indeed reorganized into a more centralized system which is often segmented into different levels. One of Napoleon's educational reforms included the establishment of lycées, which were secondary schools aimed at preparing students for service to the state. Napoleon also established primary schools in each commune and reformed higher education by creating specialized institutions such as the École Polytechnique. The system was aimed at creating a trained and literate bureaucracy and military officer corps loyal to him.

The storming of the Bastille led to the spread of violence across the English country side


The storming of the Bastille led to the spread of violence across the English country side


True

False

False.

The storming of the Bastille occurred in France, not England, on July 14, 1789. It was a pivotal event in the French Revolution, symbolizing the tyranny of the Bourbon monarchy and the oppressive Ancien Régime. The event itself, as well as the revolutionary fervor that followed, led to the spread of violence across the French countryside during the period known as the Great Fear, not the English countryside. Meanwhile, in England, the reaction to the French Revolution was complex and varied, with some support for the principles of the revolution amongst radicals but also significant apprehension and opposition among conservatives and the ruling classes.

The Napoleonic Code stated that no longer would there be aby special privileges for Nobles and Churchmen.


The Napoleonic Code stated that no longer would there be aby special privileges for Nobles and Churchmen.


True

False

True.

The Napoleonic Code, or Code Napoléon (officially the Code civil des Français), enacted in 1804, abolished the feudal privileges of the nobility and clergy that had been a cornerstone of the Ancien Régime. One of its central tenets was the equality of all male citizens before the law and the protection of property rights. This meant an end to the special privileges that had been enjoyed by the nobility and the higher ranks of the clergy under France's old legal system. The Code also included clear definitions of contract law and property rights, and it established a uniform set of laws across the territories controlled by Napoleon.

1 point 

 
 
Question at position 6 
After the March on Versailles, a new constitution was created in which ‘active citizens’ would allowed to vote.


After the March on Versailles, a new constitution was created in which ‘active citizens’ would allowed to vote.


True

False

True.

After the Women's March on Versailles in October 1789, which was one of the earliest and most significant events of the French Revolution, the political landscape began to shift significantly. While the new constitution wasn't framed immediately after the march, the event did contribute to the pressures leading to significant political change. 

The Constitution of 1791, which was completed and came into effect in September 1791, introduced a constitutional monarchy and provided for a Legislative Assembly. One of the key features of this constitution was the distinction between 'active' and 'passive' citizens. Only 'active' citizens, who paid a certain amount of taxes, were entitled to vote. Women, slaves, children, and foreigners, as well as men who did not pay enough taxes, were classified as 'passive' citizens and were not permitted to vote.

In 1813, Napoleons first major mistake was when he invaded Russia


In 1813, Napoleons first major mistake was when he invaded Russia


True

False

False.

Napoleon's invasion of Russia actually occurred in 1812. This campaign is widely considered one of his greatest military blunders as the Grande Armée, which was initially over 600,000 strong, was decimated by the Russian winter, disease, and continuous Russian tactics of retreat and scorched earth. The disastrous result of this campaign was a pivotal point in the Napoleonic Wars, significantly weakening Napoleon's forces and prestige and leading to a surge in opposition against him. The Battle of Leipzig, also known as the Battle of Nations, which took place in 1813, was another significant defeat following the invasion of Russia, where the coalition forces decisively defeated Napoleon's army.

The main goal of the Congress of Vienna was to ensure that no person would come close to taking over Europe again


The main goal of the Congress of Vienna was to ensure that no person would come close to taking over Europe again


True

False

True.

The main goal of the Congress of Vienna (1814–1815) was to establish a balance of power in Europe that would prevent any single nation or individual from dominating the continent, as Napoleon Bonaparte had nearly done. The congress aimed at creating lasting peace by redrawing the map of Europe and settling issues arising from the French Revolutionary Wars, the Napoleonic Wars, and the dissolution of the Holy Roman Empire. To this end, the leaders and diplomats at the Congress of Vienna created a framework for European international politics that led to a period known as the "Concert of Europe," in which the major powers worked together to maintain stability and prevent major conflicts.

﻿Place the description of the way government was handled in France under the King, Revolution and Napoleon
Under the King
No Answers Chosen
Under Napoleon
No Answers Chosen
During The Revolution
No Answers Chosen
Possible answers
No National Assembly
All Men could vote
No single ruler
A National Assembly was elected by Active Citizens
Two National Assemblies
Absolute power of the monarch and he could not be removed

Under the King:
- Absolute power of the monarch and he could not be removed

During The Revolution:
- A National Assembly was elected by Active Citizens
- All Men could vote (Note: This is true but requires clarification; 'All Men' refers to all 'active citizens,' as defined by the 1791 Constitution, and not literally all men as women, those without property, and other groups were excluded.)
- No single ruler (The Revolution went through various phases, including a constitutional monarchy and a republic with multiple governing bodies like the National Convention and the Committee of Public Safety.)

Under Napoleon:
- No National Assembly (Initially, there were legislative bodies, but Napoleon held most of the power and they had very limited roles.)
- Two National Assemblies (During the period of the Consulate, following the Constitution of the Year VIII, there were the Tribunat and the Corps Législatif, but they did not have true legislative power, which was concentrated in the hands of Napoleon as First Consul and then as Emperor.)

Which leader listed below is most closely associated with the Reign of Terror?


Which leader listed below is most closely associated with the Reign of Terror?


Louis XIV


Napoleon Bonaparte


Maximillen Robespierre


Jack Obin

Maximilien Robespierre is the leader most closely associated with the Reign of Terror. 

During the French Revolution, Robespierre was a prominent member of the Committee of Public Safety and became one of the principal architects of the Reign of Terror, a period from 1793 to 1794 where tens of thousands of people were executed by guillotine under suspicion of counter-revolutionary activities.
